disclaimer: Manual end-of-day snapshot compiled from public sources for informational purposes only — not validated for trading or investment decisions.
marketPulse: Powell's Final Act: Fed Holds, Brent Hits $120, Alphabet Delivers Historic Cloud Beat in 80-Second Earnings Sprint
marketPulseSubtext: The S&P 500 closed flat (-0.04% to 7,135.95) and the Nasdaq barely positive (+0.04% to 24,673.24) while the Dow slumped 280 points (-0.57% to 48,861.81) as oil surged to its highest since 2022. Brent settled at $118.03 and briefly hit $120.27 after Trump told aides to prepare for an extended Iran blockade. Then after the bell, four hyperscalers reported in 80 seconds: Alphabet delivered the blowout of the quarter with Google Cloud +63%, while Meta missed on users and dropped AH, Microsoft beat narrowly, and Amazon guided well above expectations. VIX closed at 18.38 as the session ended with more questions answered than raised.
topDriver: Brent hits $118 / $120 intraday on extended blockade signal — then Alphabet's 63% Cloud beat partially offset by Meta's AH drop
narrativeSummary: April 29 was Powell's farewell and the market's most consequential 80 seconds of the year. During the session, oil dominated: Brent surged 4.74% to settle at $118.03 — its highest settlement since June 2022 — and briefly printed $120.27 after the Wall Street Journal and Axios both reported Trump has told aides to prepare for an extended Iranian blockade. WTI surged nearly 7% to $106.88, its first close above $100 in three weeks. The Fed held rates as expected, but the statement was notable for four dissenters and explicit language acknowledging Middle East uncertainty and rising energy prices. Powell confirmed he will stay on the Fed Board after his chairmanship ends May 15, called it his 'last press conference as chair,' and said Trump's legal attacks have 'left me no choice' but to resort to the courts to defend Fed independence. After the bell, four hyperscalers reported in approximately 80 seconds. Alphabet was the undisputed star: Google Cloud grew 63% to $20B, revenue hit $109.9B vs $107B expected, and EPS came in at $5.11 against a $2.63 consensus — shares rallied 4%+ in after-hours. Microsoft beat cleanly with Azure growth strong and 20M Copilot seats. Amazon guided Q2 at $194-199B vs $188.9B expected, with capex of $44.2B confirming AI infrastructure commitment. Meta beat on revenue ($56.3B vs $55.45B) but raised capex to $125-145B from $115-135B and missed user growth — stock fell in AH trading. Net read: the AI capex thesis is alive and the OpenAI revenue scare was sector-specific, but Meta's user miss injected a new concern about social-AI monetization. Apple reports Thursday; GDP and PCE tomorrow.
macroStressSubtext: Brent hits $120, extended blockade signal, 10Y jumps to 4.41% — energy shock entering critical phase
rateCurveRead: The 10Y jumped more than 5 basis points to 4.41% after the Fed decision as the market interpreted the four-dissenter statement and Powell's hawkish inflation language as reinforcing 'higher for longer' — with Brent at $118 making rate cuts essentially impossible in 2026. The 2Y rose to 3.88% while the 30Y held at 4.97%. Wells Fargo's Luis Alvarado said 'the bar for cutting rates has risen meaningfully' given global energy prices. The curve remains in bear-steepener territory: the front end is pinned by Fed-on-hold expectations while the long end continues to drift higher on energy-driven inflation risk. Matt Rowe of Man Group: 'The market is telling you the inflation story is likely to persist into next year, and likely that means raising rates before cutting them.'

scenarios.label: Bear
scenarios.title: Extended Blockade + Meta User Decay Spreads
scenarios.trigger: Extended Iran blockade confirmed; Brent holds above $120; Meta's user miss spreads to ad revenue concerns at GOOGL; Apple disappoints Thursday; VIX breaks 22
scenarios.outcome: Oil above $120 forces a 'stagflation' market repricing: 10Y pushes toward 4.6%, growth multiples compress, the Fed is boxed in unable to cut or hike. Meta's user miss is confirmed as a broader social-AI monetization problem. Apple's guidance is soft on consumer weakness. Dow leads the selloff as consumer and industrial stocks crack.

scenarios.label: Base
scenarios.title: AI Capex Validated, Oil Stays Volatile, Apple Muddies Waters
scenarios.trigger: Alphabet's 63% Cloud beat sets the tone; Microsoft and Amazon confirm AI demand; Apple beats but guides cautiously; Brent stays $110-120; 10Y stabilizes at 4.40-4.45%
scenarios.outcome: Three of four hyperscalers validate the AI capex thesis — Alphabet's blowout is the dominant read and Meta's user miss is treated as company-specific. Apple's result Thursday sets the consumer tone. Oil remains elevated but markets accept it as 'priced in.' Records hold on narrow leadership. Nasdaq recovers from AH mixed signals to grind higher next week.

scenarios.label: Bull
scenarios.title: Iran Ceasefire + AI Sweep
scenarios.trigger: Trump signals de-escalation after extended blockade signal was a negotiating tactic; Brent falls toward $100; Apple beats and raises; Alphabet's 63% Cloud growth triggers broad AI re-rating
scenarios.outcome: The combination of oil shock relief and confirmed AI monetization triggers the broadest rally of the year. Breadth explodes — software, IWM, consumer all participate. Gold rebounds as dollar eases. S&P pushes toward 7,400 within days. The 'extended blockade' signal is reframed as negotiating leverage that worked.

crossMarket.driver: S&P closed essentially flat at 7,135.95 (-0.04%), holding above 7,100 support despite Dow weakness and oil at $118. The Nasdaq's slim +0.04% gain to 24,673.24 reflected pre-earnings positioning in hyperscalers. After-hours, Alphabet's blowout sent futures higher.

Nasdaq edged up 0.04% to 24,673.24 — effectively flat during the session as hyperscalers traded cautiously ahead of results. After-hours, Alphabet +4% and Microsoft +1% partially offset by Meta -3%. Net AH direction: modestly positive. Nasdaq is up 14% in April, on track for best month since April 2020.

Dow fell 280.12 points (-0.57%) to 48,861.81 — its fifth consecutive losing day — as oil above $118 hit consumer, industrial, and transportation stocks hard. Booking Holdings -4.5% after lowering guidance citing Middle East conflict impacts through end of June.

Russell 2000 fell 1.15% — the worst performer on the day — with only 379 of its holdings advancing and 1,521 declining. Oil-driven inflation and rising yields are the primary constraint: small caps have the highest leverage exposure and weakest pricing power to pass through energy cost inflation.

Brent surged 4.74% to settle at $118.03 — highest since June 2022 — and briefly hit $120.27 post-settlement after WSJ/Axios reported Trump is preparing for an extended Iranian blockade. Eighth consecutive session of gains. EIA forecasts Brent peaking at $115/b in Q2; the market has already exceeded that.

WTI surged nearly 7% to settle at $106.88 — its first close above $100 in three weeks and the highest level since the early days of the Iran war. Trump's extended blockade signal removed any near-term de-escalation narrative. US gas average hit $4.176/gallon, highest since August 2022.

10Y jumped more than 5bps to 4.41% after the Fed decision — a one-month high — as four dissenters, explicit Middle East language in the statement, and Brent at $118 all reinforced the 'higher for longer' view. Matt Rowe of Man Group: 'The inflation story is likely to persist into next year, and likely that means raising rates before cutting them.'

2Y rose to 3.88% (+5bps) as the front end repriced slightly higher on the Fed's hawkish-leaning hold. Still anchored by the universal expectation of no rate cuts in 2026, but the extended-blockade scenario now raises the probability of rate hikes before year-end, per Wells Fargo Investment Institute.

Alphabet delivered the quarter's biggest earnings blowout: $109.9B revenue (+22% YoY) vs $107B expected, Google Cloud +63% to $20.03B vs $18.05B expected, EPS $5.11 vs $2.63 consensus (inflated by $36.9B unrealized equity gain). Shares rose 4%+ in AH. Search queries at all-time high with AI experiences. Cloud backlog nearly doubled QoQ to over $460B.

Microsoft beat on revenue ($82.89B vs $81.39B) and EPS ($4.27 vs $4.06). Azure growth exceeded consensus. 20M paid Copilot 365 commercial seats, up from 15M prior quarter. Shares slipped 1% AH — investors wanted more. Down 12% YTD but up 20%+ in April going into results.

Meta beat on revenue ($56.3B vs $55.45B) but missed user growth and raised 2026 capex to $125-145B from $115-135B. The user miss, combined with the higher capex, reopened the AI-ROI question for social platforms. Shares fell in AH trading. Zuckerberg's Superintelligence Labs bet is getting more expensive without clear user monetization proof.

Amazon beat on revenue and guided Q2 at $194-199B vs $188.9B expected — a strong beat on the top line. AWS capex hit $44.2B in Q1, above the $43.6B estimate, confirming the AI infrastructure commitment. Stock reaction mixed AH as investors parsed the capex depth versus the revenue guidance beat.

VIX closed at 18.38, up from 19.06 Tuesday — the Schwab note observed VIX fell 'below 18' Tuesday despite chip weakness, reflecting persistent complacency ahead of the hyperscaler results. Post-AH, Alphabet's blowout likely pushes VIX lower Thursday, but oil above $118 provides a floor.

drivers.name: Alphabet's Google Cloud +63% to $20B — the most important single earnings number of Q1 2026
drivers.detail: Alphabet reported Q1 revenue of $109.9B (+22% YoY) vs $107B expected. Google Cloud grew 63% to $20.03B, demolishing the 48% rate from Q4 and well ahead of the ~50% consensus. Cloud backlog nearly doubled quarter-on-quarter to over $460B. Search queries hit an all-time high with AI experiences driving usage and 19% revenue growth. EPS came in at $5.11 vs $2.63 consensus, though a $36.9B unrealized equity gain inflated the headline bottom line. Shares rose 4%+ in AH trading.
drivers.linked: GOOGL, AI infrastructure thesis, MSFT Azure, AWS, NVDA demand chain, cloud capex cycle

drivers.name: Trump tells aides to prepare for extended Iran blockade — Brent hits $118 settlement and $120.27 intraday
drivers.detail: The Wall Street Journal reported Trump told aides to prepare for an extended blockade of Iranian ports. Axios confirmed Trump rejected Iran's Hormuz reopening proposal. Brent settled at $118.03 — its highest since June 2022 — and briefly hit $120.27 after settlement. WTI surged nearly 7% to $106.88. US average gas prices hit $4.176/gallon, highest since August 2022. The EIA had forecast Brent peaking at $115/b in Q2; the market has already exceeded that by a meaningful margin.
drivers.linked: Brent, WTI, energy stocks, inflation expectations, 10Y yield, consumer spending, airline and transportation margins

drivers.name: Fed holds, 4 dissenters, Powell's final presser — explicit Iran/energy uncertainty language in statement
drivers.detail: The Fed held the federal funds rate unchanged as universally expected. The statement acknowledged 'developments in the Middle East are contributing to a high level of uncertainty about the economic outlook.' Four governors dissented — three wanted to remove language hinting at future easing, one (Miran) wanted a 25bp cut. Powell confirmed he will stay on the Fed Board after May 15 despite Trump's legal pressure. Kevin Warsh cleared the Senate Banking Committee 13-11 on party lines earlier in the day. Analysts at Natixis warned Warsh will be 'the least influential Fed chair in a long time' given the committee's anti-cut consensus.
drivers.linked: Fed funds rate, 2Y Treasury, 10Y Treasury, Warsh transition, rate-sensitive sectors, USD

drivers.name: Meta revenue beat but user miss and capex raised to $125-145B — social AI monetization under pressure
drivers.detail: Meta posted Q1 revenue of $56.3B vs $55.45B expected — a beat — but missed on user growth figures and raised 2026 capex guidance to $125-145B from $115-135B, the steepest proportional step-up among the four hyperscalers. The user miss, combined with higher AI spending, reopened questions about whether social platforms can monetize AI investments as directly as cloud infrastructure players. Meta stock fell in AH trading.
drivers.linked: META, digital advertising, AI ROI for social platforms, GOOGL YouTube comparison, Scale AI investment

drivers.name: Amazon Q2 guided $194-199B vs $188.9B — AWS demand and retail resilience confirmed
drivers.detail: Amazon reported Q1 results with Q2 guidance of $194-199B — a strong beat vs the $188.9B consensus. AWS capex reached $44.2B in Q1, slightly above the $43.6B estimate, confirming continued AI infrastructure commitment. Andy Jassy confirmed OpenAI models coming to AWS Bedrock imminently. The revenue guidance beat is significant given the oil-shock consumer backdrop.
drivers.linked: AMZN, AWS, cloud infrastructure, AI model distribution, retail margins, consumer spending

risks.title: Brent at $118 and briefly $120 — the EIA's Q2 'peak' forecast is already breached
risks.desc: The EIA's April STEO forecast called for Brent peaking at $115/b in Q2. Brent settled at $118.03 on April 29 and briefly exceeded $120. With Trump signaling an extended blockade, the upside scenario — J.P. Morgan's $120-130 range — is no longer tail risk. At these levels, diesel is above $5.80/gallon and gasoline is heading toward $4.30. Every 10 cent increase in gasoline costs US consumers approximately $13 billion annually in aggregate spending power.

risks.title: Meta's user miss is a new AI monetization fault line — distinct from infrastructure validation
risks.desc: Alphabet, Microsoft, and Amazon all validated AI capex through cloud revenue growth. Meta's miss is categorically different: it's a social platform that invested $14.3B in Scale AI and is raising capex to $125-145B, but its user growth is decelerating. This creates a two-tier AI narrative: infrastructure companies (GOOGL, MSFT, AMZN) are monetizing; distribution/application companies (META, and potentially others) are not yet. If this spreads to Apple's AI features Thursday, the narrative fractures further.

risks.title: 10Y at 4.41% post-Fed — Wells Fargo: rate hikes before cuts are possible
risks.desc: The 10Y rising to 4.41% after a Fed hold is a hawkish signal. Wells Fargo Investment Institute's Luis Alvarado said the bar for cutting rates 'has risen meaningfully' and that the inflation story with oil at $118 'is likely to persist into next year, and likely that means raising rates before cutting them.' Man Group's Matt Rowe echoed this. A 10Y sustained above 4.40% with oil above $110 is a structural headwind to tech multiples, housing, and consumer credit.

risks.title: Warsh transition creates Fed policy uncertainty — 'least influential chair' risk
risks.desc: Warsh cleared Senate Banking 13-11 and heads to a full Senate vote. Natixis chief economist Christopher Hodge warns Warsh will be 'the least influential Fed chair in a long time' and will face extreme difficulty building consensus for rate cuts with the existing committee. Powell's departing comment that Trump's legal attacks 'left me no choice' but to defend Fed independence in court signals the institutional tension is real and ongoing.

risks.title: Dow's fifth straight losing day — consumer and industrial breadth deteriorating under oil pressure
risks.desc: The Dow has fallen for five consecutive sessions — a streak not seen since January. Booking Holdings cut guidance citing Middle East impacts through June. Illinois Tool Works, UPS, and multiple industrials are guiding cautiously. The consumer economy is holding (Visa +6%, Starbucks +5%) but the industrial and travel sectors are signaling direct oil-shock margin damage. If this spreads to May's guidance season, the breadth picture collapses further.

timeline.headline: Kevin Warsh cleared Senate Banking Committee 13-11 on party lines. Futures flat. Oil already above $115 Brent in European hours. Visa +6% pre-market after beating on 17% revenue growth and reporting 'resilient' consumer spending.
timeline.effect: Set a mixed tone — financial sector positive on Visa, rate path hawkish on Warsh, oil market already elevated before US open.

timeline.headline: S&P opened near flat and traded in a tight range. Booking Holdings fell 4.5% on lowered guidance citing Middle East conflict through June. Starbucks +5% after beating estimates with 6.2% global same-store sales growth. Hyperscalers 1-2% lower ahead of results.
timeline.effect: Classic pre-earnings compression. Travel and leisure confirming direct oil-shock margin damage; consumer brands beating on spending resilience.

timeline.headline: Fed announced hold as expected. Statement included explicit Middle East language and four dissenters. 10Y jumped immediately to 4.41%. Oil surged as WSJ/Axios reported Trump is preparing for an extended blockade — Brent crossed $117.
timeline.effect: The Fed and Iran news hit simultaneously, a double-hawkish shock. Dow accelerated lower; Russell 2000 hit session lows. Nasdaq briefly turned negative before stabilizing.

timeline.headline: Powell: 'This is my last press conference as chair.' Confirmed staying on Board. Said Trump's legal attacks 'left me no choice' but to use courts. Congratulated Warsh. Said policy is 'well-positioned' to respond to evolving outlook.
timeline.effect: Markets treated Powell's farewell as ceremonial — the real policy direction will now shift to Warsh. Treasury yields held their Fed-decision gains. Equities drifted into close near unchanged.

timeline.headline: S&P -0.04% to 7,135.95. Nasdaq +0.04% to 24,673.24. Dow -280 to 48,861.81. Brent settled $118.03, briefly hit $120.27. WTI settled $106.88. 10Y at 4.41%. VIX 18.38.
timeline.effect: A session that felt more consequential than its flat tape suggested. The day's true verdict would come after the bell.

timeline.headline: In approximately 80 seconds: Alphabet reported $109.9B revenue, Cloud +63% to $20B, EPS $5.11 — shares +4%. Microsoft beat top and bottom line, Azure strong, +1% AH. Meta beat revenue but missed user growth and raised capex — stock fell. Amazon guided Q2 at $194-199B — mixed reaction.
timeline.effect: Net AH verdict: AI capex thesis is alive. Alphabet's blowout overwhelms Meta's user miss. S&P futures pushed higher. The OpenAI revenue scare of Tuesday is effectively answered — but Meta introduces a new question about whether social AI monetizes differently than infrastructure AI.

watchpoints.event: Apple Q1 earnings — consumer hardware demand, AI feature monetization, Tim Cook CEO transition announcement context
Q1 GDP advance estimate — first read on growth under the full weight of the Hormuz closure and oil shock
PCE inflation (March) — Fed's preferred inflation gauge; energy component will show early oil shock impact
ECB rate decision — European growth vs energy inflation; ECB faces same 'stagflation trap' as Fed
GOOGL and MSFT open — gap-up on AH beats sets early Friday tone for tech and AI narrative
UAE officially exits OPEC — watch for Saudi Arabia response and any cascading OPEC+ exits
First Warsh Fed meeting — will he attempt to build consensus for cuts or defer to committee hawkishness on oil inflation?
White House / Iran response to extended blockade signal — Trump said 'very soon'; Brent direction binary on this

strategies.title: GOOGL: Cloud 63% is Not Priced In — Gap-Up Has Legs
strategies.body: Alphabet's Google Cloud growing 63% to $20B with backlog nearly doubling to $460B is not a one-quarter anomaly — it reflects enterprise AI adoption at scale. Microsoft's 'exclusivity loss' to OpenAI is a tailwind for Google Cloud. The stock's +4% AH move likely underprices the magnitude of the beat relative to where consensus was. Gap-ups after earnings of this scale historically have follow-through.
strategies.trigger: GOOGL opens above prior close on Thursday with volume confirmation; Google Cloud Q2 guidance implies continued 50%+ growth

strategies.title: Meta User Miss: Differentiate Social AI from Infrastructure AI
strategies.body: Meta's miss creates a useful distinction: cloud infrastructure AI (GOOGL, MSFT, AMZN) is monetizing through enterprise compute demand. Social AI (META) is monetizing more slowly because user engagement with AI features doesn't translate directly to ad impressions. If Apple's AI features also disappoint on engagement Thursday, this becomes a 'distribution vs. infrastructure' thesis that reshapes the AI landscape.
strategies.trigger: Apple's AI features show soft adoption in Thursday guidance; META fails to recover above pre-earnings close within 3 sessions

strategies.title: Oil Extended Blockade: Brent Path to $125-130
strategies.body: With Trump signaling extended blockade, the J.P. Morgan $120-130 scenario is now the working base case. Brent already hit $120.27 intraday. The EIA's $115 Q2 peak has been breached. Energy stocks still lag crude in this cycle due to production constraints behind Hormuz, but refiners and non-Gulf energy exporters may be the cleaner expression.
strategies.trigger: Extended blockade confirmed in next White House statement; Brent holds above $118 for 3 consecutive sessions

strategies.title: Rate Hike Tail Risk: Wells Fargo's 'Raises Before Cuts' Scenario
strategies.body: Wells Fargo Investment Institute and Man Group are explicitly flagging the rate hike scenario for the first time this cycle. Brent at $118 with a closed Hormuz strait could push core PCE above 3.5% in Q2 — territory where the Fed historically cannot sit idle. Duration risk across bonds and rate-sensitive equities is not priced for a potential hike path. 30Y approaching 5% would be a significant re-rating event.
strategies.trigger: PCE tomorrow surprises above 3.0% MoM; Brent holds above $115 through May; 10Y approaches 4.60%

strategies.title: Visa / Consumer Services: Resilience in a K-Shaped Economy
strategies.body: Visa surged 6%+ after reporting 17% revenue growth and calling consumer spending 'resilient.' This follows Coca-Cola's 10% organic growth, American Express's beat, and Starbucks +5% on same-store sales. The US consumer — particularly the top-income cohort — is not breaking despite oil shock. Consumer services with pricing power are the defensive play that doesn't require a bet on oil direction or AI timing.
strategies.trigger: Visa and comparable payment networks hold gains through May; retail sales data confirms upper-income spending resilient

flows.note: Largest single-day energy ETF inflow since March. Brent $118 and extended blockade signal driving institutional positioning into the inflation source directly.
Flight to short-end safety ahead of Fed decision. 4-week T-bill yield above 5.2% attracting cash parking. SPI>65 stagflation playbook: stay short duration.
SPI at 73 triggering stagflation regime positioning. Real yield protection demand rising as PCE data due Thursday and Brent at $118 keeps inflation expectations elevated.
Pre-earnings hedging on Mag-7 reporting night. OpenAI revenue miss Tuesday kept risk-off tone in growth tech through Wednesday session.
30Y at 4.97% and rising. Wells Fargo rate-hike scenario gaining credibility. Long duration is the stagflation short — classic SPI>65 positioning.
Dollar strength at 98 and rising real yields compressing the non-yielding haven bid despite geopolitical stress. Gold fell 2.1% on the session.
HY OAS spread at 412bp and widening. Credit stress building directionally — not distress territory yet, but rotation toward quality underway.
Alphabet Cloud +63% AH beat reigniting AI demand thesis. AICS at 72 and rising. Pre-positioning for SOXX recovery after 2-day pause.
BTC -1.4% tracking tech risk-off. Crypto acting as high-beta AI sentiment proxy, not a haven — declining alongside Nvidia rather than alongside gold.

flowsRead: The flow picture on April 29 confirms the SPI>65 stagflation read with unusual precision. The two largest inflows — short-term Treasuries ($3.1B) and energy ETFs ($2.8B) — are the textbook stagflation positioning: park cash at 5.2% yield and get long the inflation source simultaneously. TIPS inflows confirm the regime read is institutional, not retail noise. The outflows complete the picture: long-duration bonds (-$1.1B), gold (-$0.8B), high yield (-$0.6B), and tech (-$1.9B) are all seeing money leave in the same session. The gold outflow is the most counterintuitive signal given geopolitical stress — it says dollar strength and real yield pressure are currently winning the tug-of-war against haven demand. The modest semiconductor inflow (+$0.9B) reflects the Alphabet AH beat seeding early conviction in the AICS signal. Net read: money is rotating exactly as OGPI 78 and SPI 73 would predict.
